name: TheDream.US National Scholarship
description: Provides tuition and fees coverage up to $33,000 for a bachelor's degree, plus additional stipends of up to $6,000 for books, supplies, and transportation. Designed for first-generation immigrant students with or without DACA or TPS who came to the U.S. before age 16 and before November 1, 2020. Partners with over 80 colleges across 20 states to provide access to higher education for immigrant students who may not qualify for federal financial aid.
eligibility: First-generation immigrant students with or without DACA or TPS; must have arrived in U.S. before age 16 and before November 1, 2020; must demonstrate financial need; GPA of 2.5+ required
applicationProcess: Apply online at thedream.us; application opens November 1 annually
documentsRequired: Proof of immigration status, high school transcripts, financial documentation, personal essay

name: Paul & Daisy Soros Fellowships for New Americans
description: Prestigious graduate fellowship providing up to $90,000 over two years to immigrants and children of immigrants pursuing full-time graduate or professional degrees in the United States. The award includes $40,000 in tuition support paid directly to the university and $50,000 in stipend support paid to the Fellow. Approximately 30 fellows are selected annually from a pool of over 2,400 applicants, making it one of the most competitive and generous fellowships for New Americans.
eligibility: Must be a New American (immigrant, green card holder, naturalized citizen, or child of immigrants); age 30 or younger at deadline; must hold a bachelor's degree; pursuing or applying for full-time graduate study
applicationProcess: Apply online at pdsoros.org; application deadline October 29, 2026 for 2027 cycle; requires essays, transcripts, CV, and 3-5 recommendations
documentsRequired: Two essays, resume/CV, transcripts, 3-5 letters of recommendation, proof of New American status

name: UNHCR DAFI Tertiary Scholarship Programme
organization: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR)
description: The Albert Einstein German Academic Refugee Initiative (DAFI) is the flagship higher education scholarship programme of UNHCR, funded by the German government. It enables refugees to pursue undergraduate degrees in their host countries by covering tuition, learning materials, and a monthly living allowance. Since 1992, DAFI has supported over 23,000 refugee students in more than 50 countries, providing access to quality higher education that would otherwise be out of reach for displaced populations.
eligibility: Recognized refugees with UNHCR refugee status; must reside in a host country with DAFI-partner institutions; under 28 years of age; must have completed secondary education
amount: Full tuition, learning materials, and monthly living allowance (varies by country)
applicationProcess: Apply through local UNHCR offices or partner organizations in host country; application periods vary by country
documentsRequired: UNHCR refugee documentation, secondary school certificates, academic transcripts, proof of residence in host country

name: College Assistance Migrant Program (CAMP)
organization: U.S. Department of Education, Office of Migrant Education
description: Federal program providing academic and financial support to students from migrant and seasonal farmworker families during their first year of undergraduate study. CAMP grants are awarded to institutions of higher education which then provide eligible students with tutoring, counseling, stipends, and other support services. The program helps bridge the gap for migrant students transitioning to college by covering costs beyond what financial aid provides and offering academic mentoring.
eligibility: Must be from a migrant or seasonal farmworker family; must be enrolled or accepted at a CAMP-funded institution; must be in first year of undergraduate study; U.S. citizen or eligible non-citizen
amount: Varies by institution; covers tuition gaps, stipends, and support services
applicationProcess: Apply through participating CAMP-funded colleges and universities; contact institution's CAMP office directly
documentsRequired: Proof of migrant/farmworker family background, college admission letter, FAFSA completion, academic records

name: High School Equivalency Program (HEP)
organization: U.S. Department of Education, Office of Migrant Education
description: Federal program helping migrant and seasonal farmworkers and their families obtain a high school equivalency diploma (GED or equivalent). HEP provides instructional services, counseling, health services, stipends, and placement assistance. The program is administered through grants to institutions of higher education and nonprofit organizations, and serves as a critical pathway for farmworker families to achieve educational credentials needed for better employment and further education opportunities.
eligibility: Must be a migrant or seasonal farmworker or dependent family member; age 16 or older; not currently enrolled in school; lack a high school diploma or equivalent
amount: Free program — covers instruction, materials, testing fees, and provides stipends
applicationProcess: Contact local HEP-funded institutions; no direct federal application; programs are run through colleges and nonprofits
documentsRequired: Proof of migrant/farmworker background, age verification, proof of lack of high school diploma

name: Maryland Education and Training Scholarships for New Americans
organization: Maryland Department of Labor, Division of Workforce Development and Adult Learning
description: State-funded program providing education and training scholarships specifically for immigrants and refugees settling in Maryland. The New American Workforce Program covers costs for English language classes, vocational training, professional certification programs, and credential evaluation services. It helps new Americans gain the skills and certifications needed to enter the U.S. workforce, including support for translating and validating foreign-earned credentials.
eligibility: Must be a legal immigrant, refugee, or asylee residing in Maryland; must demonstrate intent to enter workforce; English language proficiency requirements may vary by program
amount: Varies by program — covers tuition for ESL, vocational training, and certification costs
applicationProcess: Apply through Maryland American Job Centers or contact the Division of Workforce Development directly
documentsRequired: Immigration documentation, Maryland residency proof, employment authorization, education/credential records

name: Phoenix Fellowship for Refugee Students
description: UK-based fellowship supporting refugee students pursuing careers in medicine and sciences. Provides up to 20,000 GBP per year in living support to help cover costs that university bursaries and scholarships may not address. The fellowship focuses particularly on supporting young women refugees entering STEM and medical fields, recognizing the unique barriers displaced students face in accessing higher education in healthcare and scientific disciplines.
eligibility: Must be a refugee or asylum seeker residing in the UK; pursuing undergraduate or postgraduate studies in medicine, sciences, or STEM fields; priority given to young women
applicationProcess: Applications open February-March annually for September intake; apply through Phoenix Fellowship website
documentsRequired: Proof of refugee or asylum seeker status, university admission offer, academic transcripts, personal statement
